retiredReason: Formula. Out of scope for v1 — RECOVER for a calendar.

events.note: The same greeting serves both languages, but the two countries pick the date on different principles — Mexico fixes it to the 10th, Spain and Italy use Sundays.

The same greeting serves both languages, but the two countries pick the date on different principles — Mexico fixes it to the 10th, Spain and Italy use Sundays.

bridge.note: Spanish wishes you a HAPPY day. Italian hands you 'auguri' — a plural noun meaning roughly 'good wishes', which Spanish has no single word for. Auguri covers birthdays, name days, Christmas, promotions and Mother's Day alike; it is the most useful word Spanish speakers never reach for.

interference.es_to_it: Spanish speakers construct 'felice festa della mamma' by translation. It is understood and nobody says it. Italians say: auguri.
interference.it_to_es: 'Augurios' is not Spanish. Use feliz día — Spanish has no one-word equivalent and must name the occasion.
